content:
Member-only story

Bun

JavaScript

Rust

Typescript

Developer Tools

The nine-line Bun API that calls Rust and C directly.

8 min read
Just now

--

In November 2025, I was still hand-rolling a Node-API addon just to call one function in a C library. A .node binary, a binding.gyp file, a prebuild step for three architectures, and a Slack thread with a colleague who'd hit the exact linker error I was staring at. Two hours, for one function.
The bottleneck in “just rewrite the hot path in Rust” was never the rewrite. It was the wiring. You pay a build-tooling tax to get a fast language talking to a slow one, and most JavaScript developers quietly decide the tax isn’t worth it — so the C library sits there, unused, and the slow path stays slow.
Bun’s answer to that tax is a module called bun:ffi, and it's been sitting in Bun's docs, mostly ignored, since Bun 1.0. It lets you call into a compiled library written in C, Rust, Zig, C++, C#, Nim, or Kotlin — anything with a C ABI — directly from a .ts file. No node-gyp. No .node binary. No prebuild matrix.
I have not shipped bun:ffi in anything that charges a customer money yet. I want to say that upfront, because Bun's own documentation says the same thing in its own words, and I'd rather you hear the caveat from me before you hear it from a failed production deploy.
